data.title: Intapp: A Buy With A Strong Caveat About Insider Selling
data.description:
Thesis

I rate Intapp, Inc. (NASDAQ:INTA) as Buy, with an expected 18% upside over the next 12 months, based on reasonable assumptions.

The positive aspects of the thesis are: the company possesses several competitive advantages, including its vertically-integrated products that are highly specialized for different industries. Despite there is some deceleration in revenue growth, the company still has room to grow at a more modest rate. The industry and the addressable market have a strong outlook. Additionally, the company has demonstrated good performance in key metrics like NRR and ARR, and its rapidly improving margins open the door for significant EPS growth ahead.

However, there are some negative issues to consider. Insider selling, fierce industry competition, and stock-based compensation (at 17% of total revenues) are all potential concerns. The auditor, despite giving a 'clean rating', noted a 'Critical Audit Matter'.

Business Overview and Latest Results

Intapp, which stands for 'intelligence applied', is a vertically integrated AI-powered company that offers different kinds of solutions. For instance, DealCloud is a CRM with additional features. Regarding compliance, it offers several solutions such as Intapp Conflicts, Intapp Intake, Intapp Terms, and Intapp Walls. Also, it has apps to bill and to track time (Intapp Billstream and Intapp Time), as well as collaboration and workplace solutions.

Additionally, the company differentiates itself by offering 'tailor-made' products highly specialized for specific industries such as legal, investment banking, private capital, and, most recently, real estate, following their recent acquisition of TermSheet on April 21. This high degree of specialization allows them to be more competitive than generic products of companies like SAP or Salesforce.

Regarding their segments, we note several things in the table below. The company is in a healthy transition to a SaaS-type. At the end of the 2023 FY, the SaaS segment represented just 56.2% of total revenues, and now, at the end of the 2025 fiscal year, the same segment grew to 65.8% (rounded to 66%) of total sales. This is positive because subscription revenues are more predictive than license revenues. Also, the SaaS segment is the main one to watch, because it is and will be the most important driver of value, in my opinion.

Second, while there has been some deceleration in growth Y/Y, both in revenues and in gross profit. Lastly, in third place, there is a slight improvement in margins, both in SaaS margin and in gross profit margin.

When evaluating key metrics, Net revenue retention (NRR) may be the most important. As a rule of thumb, SaaS companies should have an NRR of at least 100%; a level below that could suggest that future growth may be jeopardized.

The company's NRR of 120% demonstrates strong performance compared to its benchmarks. Scalecrush states the following:

"The average net retention rate for all SaaS companies in 2023 is 102%, which is the same as the previous year. To achieve a median growth rate of 34%, companies should aim for a net retention rate of at least 100%."

Additionally, according to SaaStr, the top NRR companies range between 120% and 140%. Therefore, Intapp should be rated in the 'Tier 1' category for this metric.

Total annual recurrent revenue (ARR) grew 22.4% Y/Y on FY24 and 20.1% FY25. Cloud-based ARR grew 33.3% and 29.4% Y/Y over the same two periods, respectively.

On August 7, the company's board authorized a new $150mm worth stock repurchase program, which represents around 7.9% of the current market cap. So, we should expect an increase in EPS by around this percentage, assuming all other variables remain constant.

Outlook, Drivers, and Industry

For the next fiscal year, the company's guidance projects total revenues between $566.7mm and $570.7mm, reflecting a 12% to 13% Y/Y growth. This guidance also includes Non-GAAP diluted EPS of $1.09 to $1.13, which would correspond with a P/E ratio of roughly 42x. From another angle, this implies a net margin expected between 15% and 15.7% (Non-GAAP).

The drivers for value creation, and especially for sales, that management puts forth are: getting new clients, expanding through existing clients (up-selling and cross-selling), and the gradual migration of industries to cloud-based systems. Also, they plan to develop new applications and solutions, enter new geographies and new sub-verticals, and pursue strategic partnerships and acquisitions.

This seems to be a sound strategy. Regarding the industry, there are two key things to be aware of, one positive and the other negative. The positive aspect is the double-digit growth expected for the industry; the negative aspect is the high and fierce competition.

Regarding the industry, the Professional Services Automation (PSA) Software industry is expected to grow at a CAGR of 11.9% from 2024 to 2030, according to GrandView Research, and at 11.32% according to Mordor Intelligence.

Valuation

Looking at valuation ratios, we may note that the stock is trading above the median sector. However, maybe one of the most critical ratios is P/E, and as I stated before, both management and analysts are expecting an EPS of $1.11~ for the next fiscal year (which in this case corresponds to the next 12 months). That gives us a forward non-GAAP diluted P/E ratio of around 40-42x.

Below, we can see the potential improvement of expected EPS relative to its prior quarters. Also, it's worth noting that we are going to find a wide range of EPS numbers, due to how they are being calculated. Especially when comparing GAAP vs Non-GAAP/Normalized and Basic vs Diluted. Keep in mind also the repurchase program that will affect EPS by roughly 7%.

Regarding absolute valuation, the model's main assumptions are: a 10% CAGR for sales growth over the next 10 years, and a net margin that will reach 22% by the tenth year. As previously discussed, management and WS analysts expect an implied profit margin of 15% (Non-GAAP) for the next fiscal year. Also, I assume a normal P/E ratio of 25, which is slightly above the technology sector's historic average of 24x. I believe this company should be slightly above the average, as I discussed previously in the NNR metric.

Based on these assumptions, the model suggests an upside of 18% over the next 12 months. The sensitivity analysis below shows how other alternative assumptions would affect the model's valuation.

Risks and Concerns

The main risk is competition. They're competing partially against giants like Salesforce and SAP, but maybe it has not been a 'direct competition' since these companies have more generic (horizontal-integrated) products, while Intapp's ones are more specialized (vertically-integrated). But now, they are in their own way to acquire and develop more vertically-integrated solutions, such as Salesforce Industry with their acquisition of Vlocity in 2020. SAP, on the other hand, launched BTP in 2021.

Additionally, there are other minor but more "direct" competitors, which specialize in the same industries as Intapp, such as Aderant, Elite from Thomson Reuters, and Litera, to name a few. Also, as we all know, the software industry is changing almost every day, and the threat of new competing products is latent.

The second concern is insider selling. Over the past six months, insider ownership has decreased by 9.12% (912 basis points) according to Finviz, bringing the total stake to 9.93%. These transactions include the CEO's and CFO's.

This trend is concerning for two main reasons. The first is the magnitude of the reduction (a 9% decrease in six months). Second, the selling is not limited to one or two individuals, but involves at least six key insiders selling within the same month.

We also note that they have been steadily selling in the last 12 months. While this may not undermine a trading-buy thesis, it would call a long-term investment thesis into question.

Another concern to note is from the auditor, Deloitte. While the firm issued a 'unqualified opinion' (a clean rating), they noted a 'Critical Audit Matter' due to the high complexity of the company's contracts. Although this is not necessarily a negative and may be common in SaaS firms, it is something to be aware of. As cited in the last 10-K (p.63):

"The majority of the Company’s contracts contain multiple performance obligations. Revenue recognition for contracts with multiple performance obligations requires management judgment, especially in identifying and evaluating the various non-standard terms and conditions in the Company’s contracts with its clients and their effect on reported revenues"

Finally, stock-based compensation [SBC] at 17% of revenues is a point of consideration. While this level may be common for SaaS companies, seeking to "attract and retain talent". For example, SBC for S&P 500 companies averaged 1.2% of revenues, and it was 6.8% for the Nasdaq in 2024. While this doesn't affect the thesis based on diluted EPS, we should know this about the company.

Conclusion

Insider selling is a concern, but they still hold a 9% ownership. Also, despite there is a slight deceleration in revenues and key metrics, the company and industry suggest significant growth ahead.

Regarding relative valuation, the stock's ratios trading significantly above the sector median suggest it may be expensive at first glance. However, earnings will be a key driver in the coming quarters and could justify a higher valuation. This is why I chose an earnings-based model for my analysis.

In conclusion, despite the relevant caveats, I see an appealing 18% potential upside, which leads me to rate the stock as a Buy.

data.title: UTF: 6.9% Infrastructure Yield? You Can Do Better
data.description:
With the Trump 2.0-induced energy transition, rising U.S. power demand driven by AI growth, and half of the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law still unspent, the U.S. infrastructure sector is set for massive growth in the coming years.

Investors, whether aiming for capital returns or income, should seriously consider gaining exposure to the infrastructure industry. If you are reading this investment thesis, you have likely already invested or are seriously considering a position in the infrastructure industry.

ETFs would be particularly useful for capturing potential returns over the coming years as they remove the challenge of stock picking. That said, investors should not invest blindly in any infrastructure fund; meticulous analysis is necessary to make the right decision.

The Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (NYSE:UTF) is one such fund, which I will evaluate here. Although this fund has existed for over 20 years, it may not be the best choice for gaining exposure to the infrastructure sector due to its modest returns, high management fees, poor portfolio construction, and other drawbacks that I will cover in this thesis.

The Basics

According to UTF’s website: “The primary investment objective of the Fund is total return, with an emphasis on income through investment in securities issued by infrastructure companies.”

Page 1 of the fund’s prospectus further states that the fund will rely on fundamental analysis to select companies, implying that UTF is an actively managed fund.

UTF also uses leverage of 29.73% (which is relatively high in my view) and has a yield of 6.91% as of July 31, 2025. Two figures that stand out, not favorably, on their webpage are the managed assets expense ratio at 2.77% and the common assets expense ratio at 3.97%.

As an investor, the key figure is the common assets expense ratio of 3.97% because it reflects the total expenses applied to each share held – money out of your pocket. This number should be deducted from total returns to determine the net return on invested capital.

Performance Analysis

Morningstar numbers indicate that UTF’s total return YTD has been 16.05%. After accounting for the 3.97% expense ratio, this equates to 12.08%. Returns over the past 3 and 5 years were 4.36% and 7.65%, respectively - that's gross returns, not net after management fees and expenses.

Compared to the broader market numbers, represented by SPY with an expense ratio of just 0.095%, UTF’s performance has been weak to say the least.

For investors, this means that allocating to SPY instead of UTF, over the long-haul, would have resulted in a higher account value over the same period. Numbers tell the truth, plain and simple.

A Curious Turn

Let’s broaden our scope and compare the fund’s performance with that of the broader market since UTF’s inception.

A curious anomaly that stands out is that UTF was comfortably beating the market until the start of the pandemic but suffered a mammoth drawdown when the pandemic hit and has yet to catch up.

What happened? Why has UTF been struggling since the start of the pandemic?

If we look at the top 10 holdings of the fund and their respective performance since January 2024, it can be said that management has done a strong job in picking stocks.

Combined, the top 10 holdings have an average return of ~38%. This also explains why the fund performed well last year and outperformed the broader market.

NextEra Energy (NEE) ~33%
NiSource (NI) ~68%
TC Energy (TRP) ~31%
American Tower (AMT) ~13%
National Grid (NGG) ~13%
Duke Energy (DUK) ~36%
PPL Corporation (PPL) ~38%
Southern Company (SO) ~42%
Dominion Energy (D) ~36%
Enbridge (ENB) ~38%

(Note: returns listed below are close approximations of price returns, starting January 2024 until present)

That, however, was not the case in the preceding years. The fund’s shareholder reports provide information on the top 10 holdings for the periods ending December 31, 2021 (page 10), June 30, 2022 (page 7), December 31, 2022 (page 10), and June 30, 2023 (page 8).

One can observe that the top 10 holdings did not change substantially, and the fund held on to significant losers such as Canadian Railways (CNI) and Norfolk Southern Corp (NSC) longer than was prudent.

If we extrapolate that loosely estimated 80% success rate on the entire portfolio of equities, we still don’t have a complete explanation for the fund’s lackluster performance after the pandemic.

I believe the answer lies in the fund’s allocation outside of equities, primarily investments in corporate bonds, including junk bonds - which make 14.89% of total portfolio allocation as of June 30, 2025.

Additionally, according to its prospectus on page 13 the fund may invest, and is permitted to do so, around 25% of its managed assets in securities that are rated lower than ‘BBB-’ by S&P or lower than ‘Baa3’, implying that the fund invests in junk grade securities.

The last few years, since interest rate hikes, have been among the worst periods to be invested in junk-rated fixed income securities, or even investment grade, because high yields on U.S. Treasuries caused institutional and retail investors alike to shift their money to those assets instead of riskier fixed-income options.

An ETF called Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA) has suffered the same fate and, in my view, will likely continue to do so for the foreseeable future.

Income Is A Concern

The fund’s NAV chart since inception is concerning. It started at ~$19 and currently stands at ~$25 - an increase of only about 3.5% over 20 years. Despite this, the fund has maintained its distribution rate at above 6% for the past 10 years.

Given the total returns curve of the fund has been upward sloping since inception this could only mean that the fund has not generated sufficient income to fully support those distributions. In other words, it has relied on capital gains and investor capital to maintain a high yield.

That assertion is further supported by the fund’s Section 19 notices. In 2025, UTF generated sufficient income (over 95% of total distributions) only for the months of March and May, while the remaining months relied on capital and/or capital gains to cover the shortfall in income.

Does Not Diversify

Many investors may assume that a fund with “infrastructure” in its name would provide added exposure to the infrastructure sector while diversifying risk away from a total market fund such as SPY.

That is not the case with UTF, as it demonstrates a beta of 1.03, which is largely in line with the market. If an asset does not show movements decoupled from the broader market, it does not function as a diversification or risk-reduction tool. For more on this concept, refer to this explanation and appendix in this article.

Possible Risk Magnification

The fund’s sector composition shows it is heavily weighted toward the electricity sector with 33.91% of total funds allocated towards electric companies.

Given that AI data centers will be a major driver of energy demand in the coming years, it is reasonable to say that UTF is indirectly exposed to the AI industry.

This exposes the fund to the volatility of the technology sector. Technology is known for higher volatility compared to the broader market - QQQ has a standard deviation of 18.7, while SPY’s is 15.12.

A higher risk profile would not be a concern if returns were stronger - i.e., better on a risk-adjusted basis- but as stated earlier, UTF has underperformed the broader market and, as will be show in the next section, another infrastructure fund as well.

A Better Alternative

Based on the information presented, I believe that is is reasonable to state that UTF appears to be a poorly constructed fund. It is overly concentrated in the electricity sector and using junk-rated fixed income assets. Any alternative should first address those weaknesses.

The Global X U.S. Infrastructure Development ETF (PAVE) meets that initial criterion.

According to its website: “The Global X U.S. Infrastructure Development ETF (PAVE) seeks to invest in companies that stand to benefit from a potential increase in infrastructure activity in the United States, including those involved in the production of raw materials, heavy equipment, engineering, and construction.”

PAVE uses the Indxx U.S. Infrastructure Development Index as its benchmark, while the first page of PAVE’s annual shareholder report notes that the fund aims to full replicate that benchmark and is passively managed.

For those who are looking for a quick rundown of the Indxx U.S. Infrastructure Development Index's construction methodology: the index identifies industries and business segments likely to benefit from increased U.S. infrastructure investment, then includes companies with weights determined by their market capitalization. (Note: hyperlinking the benchmark index’s methodology PDF has been problematic; I advise readers to access it on their website linked above.)

Market cap-weighted indices are among my favorite, in fact my masters thesis was on the subject, because they are an effective way to gain exposure to an industry because each company’s weight reflects its relative importance or contribution to the market. This ensures that the companies you hold within the index mirror the natural structure of the overall market.

Such a methodology results in a portfolio that is heavily weighted towards the capital goods sector, which means weighted towards machinery and equipment used not only in electricity, but also in transport, mining, agriculture, infrastructure and industrial machines. Such diversification within industrials gives the ETF a diversified exposure within infrastructure.

Performance, however, is the ultimate measure, none of what I have mentioned thus far would matter if PAVE doesn’t deliver the results.

A comparison of PAVE’s total return against the broader market and UTF shows that PAVE has outperformed SPY by ~20% since inception and UTF by nearly 100%.

This outperformance comes with a higher beta of 1.32 and a standard deviation of 23.05, versus UTF’s beta of 1.03 and standard deviation of 17.69. However, PAVE benefits from a much lower management fee of 0.47%.

Closing Thoughts

Some may argue that PAVE is not designed as an income fund like UTF. That argument carries less weight, or none at all, when considering that UTF’s distributions have largely been sourced from capital gains.

In practice, an investor could sell PAVE shares for income when needed, which is not materially different from what UTF’s management has been doing since the inception of the fund.

There are, however, scenarios in which UTF could outperform in the coming years

If U.S. GDP grows steadily and interest rates are cut for precautionary reasons, signaling a strong economy. This could improve confidence in fixed income securities beyond U.S. Treasuries, leading to a recovery in the corporate bonds segment of UTF’s portfolio. Based on current trends, though, this does not appear likely in the near future.

UTFs heavy exposure to the electricity industry could helps its cause. But even still concerns around NAV degradation, high expense ratio, and risk magnification due to indirect AI exposure are not going away anytime soon.

As a result, I recommend a hold for UTF. Those who are in the red against the broader market due to UTF's poor performance over the last few years should continue to hold the fund until they see more gains from the fund's overperforming equity portfolio. And those who are looking to enter the infrastructure industry should consider other alternatives such as PAVE.

data.title: Franklin Resources Q3: Fallout From Western Asset Continues
data.description:
I rate Franklin Resources, Inc. (NYSE:BEN) as Hold, for income focused investors who are looking for long-term buy and hold investments. BEN is currently dealing with large net outflows from its Western Asset Management unit due to issues involving its former co-CIO, who received a Wells notice from the SEC and then took a leave of absence.

In my observation, management is busy highlighting other successes rather than dwelling on that situation. Still, the net results of the business declined in Q3, with more damage control in store for in future quarters. In my experience, it is better to leave a company in a wounded state alone, vs. load up on shares, hoping that the worst is over.

In looking at the last 10 years of BEN’s history, the stock has had inconsistent cash flows, relying on acquisitions for growth vs. organic growth. As one of the smaller asset managers, vs. say competitors such as BlackRock (BLK), Fidelity or T. Rowe Price (TROW), BEN does not have the momentum of fast rising stars such as Apollo (APO) that help it overcome the issues with Western Asset.

In the near future, BEN will continue to struggle in my view. Management will likely choose to focus on growth of the markets, rate cuts, and opportunities in alternatives, which are still a small player overall.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are:

  • Pays a quarterly dividend yielding 5%, going ex-dividend 9/30/2025.
  • Inconsistent distribution payouts, with unreliable income.
  • Over the past 10 years BEN has lost value on a total return basis
  • Q3 showed $17.1 billion in net-outflows from the Western Asset Management division due to the fallout of the scandal. It will continue in Q4, but not at the same rate.
  • 30% of AUM in countries outside the U.S., representing a growth opportunity to expand further internationally.
  • Management communication trying to hide poor results by sectioning off Western Asset Management, which they still own and have to cover the cost of losses.
  • Largest opportunity for growth is in alternatives, given the average high net worth client only dedicates 5% of their portfolio to the sector (should be 30%).

Full disclosure, I own shares of Western Asset Diversified Income Fund (WDI), which currently pays an 11.9% yield. I was concerned about the crisis at Western Asset, but it hasn’t seemed to affect performance at the fund.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock as a Hold (as of 8/29/2025), which supports my rating.

Global Asset Manager - Well Known Brands

Headquartered in San Mateo, California, Franklin Resources, Inc. is an asset management holding company. It’s best known for the Franklin Templeton, Western Asset Management, Legg Mason, Putnam, and Brandywine Global Investment Management brands names.

Through its subsidiaries, the firm provides its services to individuals, institutions, pension plans, trusts, and partnerships. It launches equity, fixed income, balanced, and multi-asset mutual funds through its subsidiaries. The firm invests in the public equity, fixed income, and alternative markets.

Franklin Resources, Inc. was founded in 1947 has additional offices in Calgary; Dubai; Edinburgh; Fort Lauderdale; Hyderabad, India; London; Rancho Cordova, US; Shanghai, China; Singapore; Stamford, US; and Vienna, Austria.

Q3 Earnings Results - Serious Outflows

On August 1, the company declared Q3 earnings, with the following financial highlights vs prior year ago period:

  • AUM declined -2% to $1,611.8 billion.
  • Revenue declined -4% to $1,593.2 billion.
  • Operating income declined -11% to $377.8 billion.
  • Net income declined -19% to $263.4 billion.
  • Earnings per share declined -18% to $0.49 per share.

Overall, the quarter showed losses across all key metrics. While listening to the earnings call, I couldn’t help asking myself, “are we looking at the same numbers?” while the CEO delivered an optimistic prepared statement. She was trying to separate the faulty Western Asset division, as if to say… if it weren’t for this situation, we would be doing very well. While that may be true, the company still has to deal with the fallout of the SEC and DOJ involvement, which is sinking the company every quarter.

Underperformance Vs. the Financial Sector

An investment in BEN of $10,000 over the past 10 years would have lost money as shown in the chart below, including dividends, severely underperforming the sector index as represented by Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF). An investor would have been better putting the $10,000 in cash vs. getting $9,929 back.

Valuation

Over the past 10 years, BEN has distributed a variable dividend, with an outsized special dividend in 2018 of $3.00, in comparison to the current quarterly dividend of $0.32.

Over the past 10 years or more, the annual distribution has been volatile with huge swings from an increase of 376% in one year to a cut of -73% in another. In my view, this inconsistency will keep long-term buy and hold investors away from the stock, while others may try to trade it to time a large payout. The chart below shows the annual distributions.

Competition

In a comparison to peers, BEN has the worst 10-year total return and the highest price earnings ratio.

T.Rowe Price Group, Inc. (TROW) is the largest in this set, which is ranked by market capitalization. Delivering a 4.6% dividend, the highest dividend growth of 9%, the highest ROE, and an admirable 10-year total return of 111%, the stock is attractive.

SEI Investments Company (SEIC) appears healthy, with a minimal 1% dividend, 30% ROE and 98% 10-year total return.

Invesco Ltd. (IVZ) owners of the famous Invesco NASDAQ ETF (QQQ) recently saw its performance turn around beating quarterly estimates, launching 3 new ETFs, increasing dividends, and enhancing shareholder returns through buybacks. I authored an article covering their achievements in “Invesco: Poised for Growth With 3 New ETFs.” Click on the title to read.

AllianceBernstein Holding L.P. Limited Partnership Units (AB) is the shining star of the pack with a 10-year total return of 248%. I authored an article covering their potential in “AllianceBernstein: High Distribution Yield with Growth Outlook.” Click on the title to read.

AllianceBernstein has a high distribution yield, with tax advantaged limited partner structure. The company is expanding through private markets, international opportunities, and capturing growth in high-fee segments, driving AUM and fee base growth.

Outlook

Once BEN can get beyond the Western Asset outflows, the largest opportunity for growth will be driven by their alternatives division. It is estimated in the financial planning community that the average high net worth client has only 5% of their portfolio dedicated to alternatives. The growing consensus among planners is that wealthy clients should be dedicating 30% to alternatives because they can handle the longer lock up periods in exchange for higher expected returns with lower correlation to the stock market.

On August 7th, 2025, President Trump signed an executive order allowing private equity, real estate, cryptocurrency, and other alternatives into 401Ks, a market worth approximately $12.5 trillion in retirement accounts. BEN is actively working on this opportunity for client-defined contribution plans.

The next earnings report for Q4 will be around November 3.

Risks To Rating

One of the largest risks facing the company is hidden costs due to Western Asset. While the worst is probably over, the bleeding of assets out of the tarnished brand continues.

On the other hand, BEN will eventually get out from the crisis and it’s possible that fearless investors who buy on weakness may be rewarded longer term. The company certainly generates enough cash flow to pay a decent dividend. The upside opportunity for placing their alternatives into retirement funds represents a significant expansion of the business.

Bottom Line

I rate BEN a Hold at this time while the company continues to deal with the Western Asset situation. Net outflows damaged its Q3 results, prompting management to call out less outflows going forward. The quarterly dividend yields 5%, going ex-dividend on 9/30.

Over the past 10 years, BEN has declined in total return. The future is expected to pick up as they overcome the current crisis and look forward to selling alternative funds to the $12 trillion dollar retirement industry. Investors are advised to wait longer before starting a position in BEN as they shed their issues and rebalance.

data.title: Nvidia: Not All That Glitters Is Growth
data.description:
Before starting my article, I'd like to explain the dynamic of the shares of the Nvidia Corporation (NYSE:NVDA) because the shares live exclusively on its price growth to win money: in other words, to take that big amount of money obtained with the company, you should sell it, because without robust dividends there's no choice of earning more than the volatility of the price and the sale at the right moment. This is not a critic but a mere description of the equity valuation model.

It seems difficult to replicate in the future the recent returns of NVDA. If we take a look at the last 3 years, or even 2025 (+35%) NVDA has delivered the strongest returns among the 7 big tech firms, which are locked in a frantic race to outspend each other on AI training.

If we focus on the last 3 years, the increase of its price is more than exceptional, with a 1000% increase, higher than the second in place Meta Platforms (META) that grew 364%.

But we can't hide the risk the company has regarding its volatility. During 2022 the market crushed due to the abrupt shift in the interest rate cycle (marking the fastest rate hike cycle since the 1980s) causing a great fall in tech. In 2022, NVDA fell 50%, surpassed later by Meta and Tesla (TSLA).

But there's something more important to point out, at the risk of making an improper comparison: if we take a look at the industries far from the tech, the shares of the other sectors, represented by ETFs of real estate (XLRE), industry (XLI), consumption (XLY), utilities (XLU) or the broad market index (SPY), were a safe place and caused less losses.

By the way, something similar happened during the first semester of this year, after DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) incident and the uncertainty that led China to be able to develop AI training at a lower cost than the U.S. I won't add another graphic or you'll get bored, and because I think you know this case. In summary: NVDA is awesome, in my opinion, but the risks of such rapid rate hikes are substantial losses. If we add that the company is moderating its growth, that's planning to add the inversions on AI estimated on $4 trillion on the next years, and that it trades at excessively high multiples, and I believe the margin of profit is narrowing, as I will demonstrate later in the Valuation section. In this context, the holding position seems to be the better option.

FQ2 2026 (Jul 2025), A True Operational Rally

NVDA's rally seems to have no end. The FQ2 2026 results, in my opinion, have been downright stupendous. Revenue estimates ($610 million above expectations) and EPS ($0.04 above expectations) were once again surpassed. Let's remember that these types of estimates aren't exactly easy to meet when the bar is so high. The last EPS estimate miss was during the third and fourth quarters of the challenging year of 2022, but back then the company, if lucky, was above $18. There was still a bit more to go before AI became a market craze, especially with OpenAI's breakthrough with ChatGPT in 2023, in my opinion. NVDA stock was already starting to soar that year.

Now, almost two or three years after what I consider to be the kick-off for the consolidation of Generative AI, NVDA is capturing the attention of the tech world. This past week was no exception. Total revenue was $46.7 billion, a 53% increase from $30 billion in the same period last year. EPS, meanwhile, reached $1.01, up from $0.75 in the previous quarter.

Net income also grew in the quarter to $26.4 billion (59% YoY) and the most amazing result that shows, from my perspective, the efficiency guarantee of NVDA's business, is found in the Gross Profit of 72.4%, which although it fell YoY compared to 75.1%, we are talking about the best gross margin among the 7 big tech.

The biggest operating boost came from Data Center, which grew 56% YoY to $41.1 billion, representing 90% of the company's business. This Data Center momentum, which even exceeds the year-over-year increase in total revenue, is the result of several AI projects, but especially the Blackwell infrastructure, which promises to continue revolutionizing the future of AI and reached record levels with 17% sequential growth. Blackwell was a central topic on last Wednesday's conference call, but one of the most important points I want to highlight was made by CFO, Colette M. Kress, a demonstration that the company, in my opinion, is capable of leading the way in AI training:

Blackwell has introduced a groundbreaking numerical approach to large language model pretraining. Using NVFP4 computations on the GB300 can now achieve 7x faster training than the H100, which uses FP8. This innovation delivers the accuracy of 16-bit precision with the speed and efficiency of 4 bit, setting a new standard for AI factor efficiency and scalability.

Not All That Glitters Is Gold

Okay. So far, I've tried to be clear about how fascinated I am by the profitability of NVDA's business. I consider it a leader in AI development and already a flagship American brand, showing the world that China is still far from matching the United States in this regard, at least for now. From now on, I'll focus on some signals that I believe will impact the future performance of NVDA's stock. First, I want to emphasize what I consider a clear moderation in the pace of earnings growth.

Since the beginning of 2024 we see a deceleration YoY, quarter to quarter, from a peak of 262% down to 53% in the most recent quarter reported. Of course, this doesn't mean any operational difficulty for the business. I want to be clear: this is purely logical if we understand that the AI boom caused hypergrowth for the company, on top of the fact that it started from a much smaller revenue base.

At the same time, I think the signal is clear: the company is entering a phase of more stable growth, still with an incredible core of more than 50% YoY. Now, in my opinion, this isn't easy to explain on Wall Street without causing more uncertainty. The estimated for the company to the FQ2026 is $54 billion which, if it does happen, will represent an increase YoY of 54%, supporting my idea that NVDA is starting to enter a more stable growth phase.

Anyway, the company’s investment projections are taking a different path, and the advances in new and improved chips are still the top priority for the directors. During the conference call, an investment projection of $3 or $4 trillion in AI infrastructure by the end of this decade was communicated, which represents a giant sum even for the largest tech companies. I have my doubts about how to measure total shareholder return on such a massive capital inversion. This is how CEO Jen-Hsun Huang expressed it:

Over the next 5 years, we're going to scale into with Blackwell, with Rubin and follow-ons to scale into effectively a $3 trillion to $4 trillion AI infrastructure opportunity. The last couple of years, you have seen that CapEx has grown in just the top 4 CSPs by -- has doubled and grown to about $600 billion. So we're in the beginning of this build-out, and the AI technology advances has really enabled AI to be able to adopt and solve problems to many different industries.

I analyzed the issue of CAPEX in the AI ​​race in a recent article on META, and given the massive investments in AI and the increases in CAPEX (META's raises some concerns), the question of when and how much the total return on investment will be continues to raise some concerns. When I read these kinds of figures, even from a leading company like NVDA, the CAPEX/Revenue ratio doesn't make sense to me. But that's a matter of estimates, so in the meantime, let's assume the company has a CAPEX/Sales ratio of 2.76%, which is obviously high, but I don't consider it worrying for now.

In addition to the revenue moderation and the ambitious projection of increased spending on AI, I want to add some specific data from the recently reported quarter that support my idea that the future profit margin for shareholders could narrow considerably. First, Data Center revenue, which investors are so excited about, grew 56% YoY to $41.1 billion, but curiously fell short of the market's expectation of $41.3 billion. This isn't something that worries me, but it does worry me that it worries the market, excuse the redundancy.

Another aspect of some operational weakness I am observing is the FCF, which was $13 billion in the quarter, almost the same as the same period last year (Jul 2024) but significantly lower than the $26 billion of the previous quarter (Apr 2025), which is a point to keep in mind since the company depends on its cash flow for the much-desired new investments in AI.

In fact, the cash per share is quite low for a company like NVDA, at $0.62 versus $2.14 for the sector median. The same goes for the FCF per share of $2.94, while similar competitors such as Broadcom (AVGO) have a ratio of $4.84 or Qualcomm (QCOM) of $10.51. Not to mention competitors in the AI ​​​​race such as META (19.86x), Alphabet (GOOG) ($5.47x), or Microsoft (MSFT) ($9.63). Furthermore, at the same time, the P/CF is vastly overvalued, with forward ratios of 41x, not to mention the P/FCF of 61x. NVDA's overvaluation is another point that leaves me with little room for enthusiasm, and which I'll analyze in the Valuation section.

Why Buy Back Shares?

Based on this situation of relatively limited FCF compared to NVDA's expectations, it was learned that the company authorized a new share buyback program for $60 billion, which implies an intention to send a signal of confidence in its cash position. I don't doubt the company's ability to generate cash, far from it, but given the investment projections of $3 to $4 trillion in the short to medium term and a growth that is beginning to moderate in order to find a break-even point, this is clearly, from my perspective, a challenge.

But in addition to this, we have the issue of the company's exaggerated overvaluation of multiples, which places it in a rather demanding position. Does it make sense to buy at NVDA's current price levels? I want to clarify that I don't think the share buyback is wrong per se, but in a business model like the one we're analyzing, in the midst of the AI ​​race, it raises questions for me. I also want to clarify that I'm not asking the company to become a dividend king, but even so, it wouldn't hurt to strengthen its performance in this regard, even though, as I clarified at the beginning, shareholder returns are only reflected in the share price. Honestly, I would like tech companies to reconsider their dividend profile, but I fully understand that the focus is elsewhere.

I simply hope that this other aspect isn't neglected or is properly planned based on a stable, strategic capital allocation. NVDA has a value on its own and its $4 trillion market cap may be a guarantee of cash efficiency, but even so, the company has already shown worrying volatility during 2025 (also in 2022) and the cash position should not be neglected, in my opinion.

Valuation

Regarding the company's valuation, I think it's evident from its multiples that the market is placing a very high premium on future earnings prospects, with a P/S of nearly 30x, a P/E that at best trades at 40x, and an EV/EBITDA of nearly 50x. These are very strong multiples. The PEG is the best performer, but considering all the other ratios, at most I can conclude that this is a fair valuation.

Even so, to assess NVDA's future performance, I developed several DCF models, one of which assumes the company's continued extraordinary profit levels, which seems highly unlikely to me, but I incorporated it nonetheless to understand how far the stock's performance could go. The company's EBIT CAGR over the last five years was an extraordinary 90%, which can only be explained by the AI boom, and where growth is already expected to moderate logically by 2025. But even so, in my first model, I applied a relatively similar 80-85% CAGR.

I'm using a 15% tax rate, the most recent published by the company, a perpetual growth rate of 2.5%, similar to the global average, and a relatively high WACC of 13% given the dominance of the cost of equity. I'm using a price of $180, 24.4 billion shares outstanding, and an initial EBIT of $102 billion based on first-half 2025 results of nearly $50 billion. In the first, super-optimistic model shown below, the upside is 31.5%, a rate that, over 5 years, would be completely incomparable to the price increase of the previous 5 years.

In the second model, with the same parameters as above, but with a 5-year EBIT CAGR of 70%, the upside narrows to 9.9%, with a target price of $197. In my opinion, this is still an optimistic scenario, perhaps more realistic than the previous one, but it is still bullish.

In the third model, I show a scenario that, in my opinion, is more realistic, with a 5-year CAGR moderating after the previous boom, to levels of 50-55%. The problem here is that the upside plummets -30%. That is, if NVDA continues to have healthy operating margins (no one could say that a 5Y CAGR of 50% is unhealthy), even so, given the expectations that the AI ​​craze will generate for the company, the losses could be very severe.

Being a good investor, I consider model 2 a possible scenario, but in that case, the 9.9% upside doesn't convince me. I choose to hold.

Risks

I've repeatedly addressed the issue in my tech articles about the upheaval generated by the news that DeepSeek could be achieving AI developments much more cheaply than NVDA or other big tech companies. The risk remains in this and, logically, in the export restrictions on its chips, which represent a colossal market for the company. In the conference call, they explained that they continue to advocate for the U.S. government to allow Blackwell to operate in China. Jen-Hsun Huang also spoke on this issue:

It is the second largest computing market in the world, and it is also the home of AI researchers. About 50% of the world's AI researchers are in China. The vast majority of the leading open source models are created in China. And so it's fairly important, I think, for the American technology companies to be able to address that market. And open source, as you know, is created in one country, but it's used all over the world.

The open source models that have come out of China are really excellent.

We're talking to the administration about the importance of American companies to be able to address the Chinese market. And as you know, H20 has been approved for companies that are not on the entities list, and many licenses have been approved. And so I think the opportunity for us to bring Blackwell to the China market is a real possibility. And so we just have to keep advocating the sensibility of and the importance of American tech companies to be able to lead and win the AI race and help make the American tech stack the global standard.

The risks in this matter are short-term because it involves a large investment and lower revenue, and long-term if the restrictions continue and China manages to find alternatives to the company's offering. The latter could be the worst-case scenario for NVDA.

Conclusion

The company is, in my opinion, leading the race to best train AI and offer the best data center services worldwide. But its growth was also driven by excessive euphoria, which drove multiples so high that today I find it very difficult to find a justifiable upside to buy at current levels. I think the best option is to hold the position until I can visualize another major leap in the pace of growth or a friendlier geopolitical environment toward China.

data.title: Hims & Hers: The Dominant Health Platform Of The Future
data.description:
Thesis

Hims & Hers (NYSE:HIMS) is no longer just a DTC telehealth storefront. It is fast becoming a targeted health platform with loyal members and rising ARPU. It is also building the foundation to expand into lab testing, hormone health, and international markets. There was an uneven headline reaction to Q2, and the setup is better than it looks. Guidance stays in place, and the weight loss playbook is advancing beyond compounding.

Q2 proved the model is scaling fast

In Q2 2025, Hims posted revenue of $544.8 million (+73% YoY), net income of $42.5 million, and adjusted EBITDA of $82.2 million. Subscribers ended the quarter above 2.4 million (+31% YoY), and the company reaffirmed full-year guidance for $2.3 to $2.4 billion of revenue and $295 to $335 million of adjusted EBITDA. These are platform-level numbers and not a niche DTC business.

Monthly online average subscriber revenue grew 30% YoY to $74. That metric with steady subscriber growth is the engine of Hims’ model and it means more people spend more on more tailored care.

The weight-loss strategy is bigger than compounding

A year ago, investors were preoccupied with Hims’ access to compounded GLP-1s at a banner price starting at $199/mo with prepayment terms. Today, the site shows a tiered lineup, and it spans compounded GLP‐1s starting at $199/mo on a 12-month plan paid upfront. It also includes branded injectables like Wegovy or Zepbound at out-of-pocket prices near $1.8 to $2.0k per month and even still oral medication kits from $69/mo. That scope matters as it lets clinicians align medication type, format, and cost to the individual. It also keeps patients on the platform even as the rulebook shifts.

The FDA cleared the semaglutide shortage in February and ceased enforcement discretion for 503B compounding facilities in May. Hims recognizes limited access to compounded semaglutide, and it notes that it continues to provide certain compounded GLP-1 options consistent with legal carve-outs.

Most importantly, the experience is working. Company data show that weight loss customers on a personalized plan that includes a GLP-1 reported a 10.3% average body weight drop at six months. Roughly 90% download the app and interact about 1.5 times per week in the first three months, with an average of six clinician touchpoints. Those engagement loops include dose titration, side effect control, and habit coaching. These loops are the glue that fuels outcomes and keeps the rate.

Unit economics: 85%+ long-term retention and sub-1-year payback

Hims notes long-term online revenue retention of 85% plus for members with at least two years of duration and a CAC payback under one year. That is SaaS-style tenacity for a consumer health platform. This also helps explain why operational leverage is emerging, and marketing expense as a percent of revenue fell in Q2 versus last year, and growth still sped up.

Gross margin ticked down to 76% due to a greater mix of weight loss products, and that was expected as that specialty scales. Leverage in operations and marketing more than offset at the bottom line by driving net income and adjusted EBITDA more than twofold YoY.

Run-rate math: the hidden $2.1B subscription engine

Hims' model’s plainness is part of the appeal. Take $74 in monthly online revenue per average subscriber and apply it to a base north of 2.4 million. That suggests about $178 million per month and about $2.1 billion annualized from the online subscription engine alone. The math simply extends the company’s reported metrics, which is why the reiterated full-year revenue of $2.3 to $2.4 billion looks attainable even amid mix changes.

Building a durable moat: data, labs, and leadership

Management is purposely evolving Hims from a telehealth marketplace into a full-stack targeted health system. The roadmap includes scaling pharmacy automation and sterile injectable throughput. It also includes layering in lab testing to gather a deeper stream of structured clinical data. The app will merge specialty-specific journeys across weight, hormone, dermatology, sexual health, and mental health, so the experience feels like one seamless care stack. New technology and product leaders include CTO Mo Elshenawy and CPO Dheerja Kaur, and they were brought in to speed up that cycle. The company also highlights MedMatch, which is a matching engine that uses deidentified data to align patients and treatments with greater accuracy. This is how Hims turns scope into personalization at scale.

International is about to matter

With the acquisition of ZAVA Global, Hims has a beachhead in the UK and rails to serve Germany, France, and Ireland, and together they cover about 160 million adults. Management expects at least $50 million of revenue from ZAVA over the rest of 2025 at a positive adj. EBITDA margin, positions it internationally as having a greater than $1 billion annual revenue potential over time. Canada is targeted as the first of several new markets, and Hims begins with generic semaglutide in 2026. International is not a deck fantasy, and it is beginning to show up in results, and it still has a route to real scale.

Valuation: growth at a reasonable multiple for a category leader

As of now, HIMS changes hands around the mid-40s with a market value near $11 billion. Netting out roughly $1.1 billion of cash and about $1.0 billion of convertible notes leaves an EV around $11 billion. On the midpoints of 2025 guidance, the stock trades at about 4.7x EV to Sales and about 35x EV to adjusted EBITDA. That is rich at first glance, and it is offset by projected revenue growth near 56 to 63 percent this year and rising ARPU and SaaS style retention. If management keeps compounding members and MORPS while it expands into labs, hormone health, and new regions, then those multiples can contract fast.

The company posted negative FCF in Q2, and that was driven by working capital builds and pharmacy automation capital spend. Despite this, it expects those working capital dynamics to settle in the second half. A $100 million buyback plan with about $65 million remaining at quarter's end provides flexibility to counter stock-based compensation or to act on dislocations. Liquidity appears sound with more than $1.1 billion in cash and equivalents.

What bears are missing

After the report, some headlines stressed a revenue miss and concerns over members and shares wavered. That is fair, and growth rarely travels in a straight line, and the move away from broad 503B compounding access created noise. But the signal was clear, and management reaffirmed full-year guidance, MORPS sped up 30% YoY, and personalized plan adoption widened. That combination of resilient demand and richer monetization is exactly what long-term holders want to see.

There is also a habit to flatten the weight loss story into compounded GLP-1, or bust. Hims is building the care framework with provider interactions and titration, side effect control and behavior change, and app support. That range spans compounded injectables where permitted, branded treatments for those who want them, and lower-priced oral kits that keep cash-paying patients engaged. That portfolio approach safeguards ARPU and keeps the rate across regulatory cycles.

The market is only getting larger

Regardless of the near-term mix, the obesity therapeutics category is growing. Analysts now project the global weight loss drug market may hit about $150 billion by the early 2030s as supply constraints loosen and new oral agents land. Those branded cash prices near about $1,000 per month underscore why affordability and care management will remain vital. And they also show why a consumer-led platform like Hims can harvest demand from patients who will not qualify, or cannot tolerate, or do not want branded injectables.

Key risks to watch

Regulatory scrutiny is real. Reuters reports the FTC is investigating Hims’ advertising and cancellation policies. This fits a wider subscription policy sweep, and it is a headline hazard worth monitoring; any required changes could slightly alter marketing or churn patterns. On the clinical front, the return to normal of GLP-1 supply and evolving compounding rules can affect the weight loss mix and gross margin. Finally, sequential member growth may wax and wane as Hims tunes pricing, prepay plans, and marketing pace.

Catalysts through 2026

Management listed several 2025 pillars that set up the next leg. They include 85% plus long-term retention and a payback under one year, and at least $725 million of weight loss revenue for 2025, while it widens the range of personalized offerings. Add the launch of hormone health in the U.S. in 2H 2025 and lab testing to thicken data moats. And add the scale-up of ZAVA in Europe with Canada expected in 2026, and you have several routes for upside surprises even still.

Bottom line

Hims is delivering on the hard parts by building engagement, stacking retention, raising ARPU, and deeply integrating pharmacy and data to deliver precision care at consumer pace. The market’s preoccupation with compounding headlines misses the bigger picture. Hims is building an at-scale multispecialty platform with the unit math of a subscription model and the growth profile of an upstart.

data.title: Movado Group: Time To Be Cautious (Downgrade)
data.description:
Back in early June of this year, I made the decision to upgrade Movado Group, Inc. (NYSE:MOV) from a Hold to a soft Buy. The stock had fallen leading up to that point at a time when the S&P 500 (SP500) had risen. Even though the enterprise was experiencing certain weak spots, I was encouraged by its robust balance sheet. Industry data showed stabilization in the watch market. And when you add on top of that the $180.5 million in net cash the company had, which stacks up well against its market capitalization of $405.8 million, I believed that it justified an upgrade.

On August 28th, the management team at the enterprise announced financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Revenue fortunately came in above what analysts were hoping to see. However, earnings per share did fall short of expectations. Even so, the stock rose by 1.8%, before climbing even more on August 29th. Thanks to this strong performance, as well as performance leading up to this point, the stock is now up 16.9% since I upgraded it. That compares to the 8.1% rise that the S&P 500 experienced over the same window of time.

However, while the stock is still attractively priced, I am becoming increasingly concerned about its financial position. This is solely because of the tariff situation that is going on and the impact it will likely have on the business moving forward. Although management is doing a good job by cutting costs, tariffs could have a meaningful impact on the business at a time when the watch industry is only just now stabilizing. And with management not willing to provide any guidance for the rest of this year, I think that downgrading it to a cautious Hold makes sense.

It wouldn't take much for me to upgrade the company once again. In fact, all it would take would be a cessation of the tariffs going on. But until that time, especially with the U.S. economy on the brink of recession, I believe that taking a cautious approach is the right decision.

Solid results… mostly

For the second quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, Movado Group actually performed quite well for the most part. Revenue for the company came in at $161.8 million. That's an increase of 3.1% compared to the $157 million the business reported one year earlier. According to management, this occurred in spite of weakness in its U.S. market. For its Watch and Accessory Brands business, revenue in the U.S. contracted from $46.5 million to $44.8 million. Management blamed this on unfavorable sales mix. This suggests to me that customers in the U.S. market are buying lower priced watches than before. None of this revenue includes activities associated with its company owned stores. In the U.S., those sales increased from $24.1 million to $24.6 million. It appears as though this was thanks to increased online outlet shopping and the opening of a new store, taking the number of retail locations the company had an operation up from 56 to 57.

When it comes to its international operations, the picture was quite a bit better. International sales for its Watch and Accessory Brands business grew from $85.1 million to $90.9 million. That's an increase of 6.8% year over year. According to the data provided, about $2.6 million of the $5.8 million increase was caused by foreign currency fluctuations that positively affected sales. However, the company also benefited from higher volumes thanks to growing demand from its wholesale customers. In particular, licensed brands for the company jumped 9.3% year over year, with some of that coming from the Americas (excluding the U.S.) and some of it coming from Europe. For its company owned stores, international revenue grew from $1.3 million to $1.5 million.

While revenue for the company came in $5 million above what analysts were hoping to see, the same could not be said of earnings per share. Although those decreased only slightly from $0.15 to $0.13, they ended up missing analysts’ expectations by $0.18. Overall net profits for the company pulled back from $3.5 million to $3 million. But the good news is that adjusted earnings per share grew from $0.15 last year to $0.23 per share this year. This took adjusted net profits up from $3.5 million to $5.3 million.

This is actually rather interesting because, according to management, the firm experienced a $2.2 million unmitigated impact from tariffs during the quarter. This is because there was a lag between the time that tariffs were implemented and the time that price increases took effect. It appears as though the business benefited from a reduction in selling, general, and administrative costs, with marketing expenses dropping $3.2 million year over year and payroll costs dropping by $2.9 million.

Other profitability metrics for the company improved without exception. Operating cash flow went from negative $17.8 million to negative $3.8 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, it grew from $9.3 million to $10.6 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company expanded from $5.3 million to $6.4 million. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2026 compared to the same time of 2025. Revenue is up thanks to the strong showing in the second quarter. However, most of the profitability metrics for the company are still down year over year, with the biggest notable exception being adjusted net profits that rose from $5.5 million to $7.2 million.

The rest of the year is going to be interesting, to say the least. My two concerns are what I'm using to justify downgrading the company at this time. The first of these is that I firmly believe the U.S. economy is going to be entering into a recession. It will likely occur this year or early next year. And most of the cause will be traced back to tariffs. These are a tax on the American people and that's the last thing that consumers need at a time when they are already struggling with a housing affordability crisis and rising food costs. Watches are not a necessary item. They are in many respects a luxury item. And while high end consumers will continue to purchase almost irrespective of how the economy is, everybody else will pause their purchases.

My other concern relates to the direct financial impact that tariffs will have on the business as opposed to just the demand side of the equation. According to management, the majority of the firm's products are sourced from Switzerland, Japan, and China. At the moment, there exists a 15% tariff on imported Japanese goods. For Chinese goods, that is 30%. And for Switzerland, which seems to be the main source of supply for the business, it is 39%.

In order to address these issues, management is taking some rather proactive steps. The first was that, on July 1st of this year, management decided to increase prices in order to mitigate the impact of tariffs. But again, this could cause demand to weaken for the company. The second thing that the business has done is to focus on cost cutting initiatives. Already this year, the company has recorded $1.5 million in accruals related to severance and employee related charges in the first half of the 2026 fiscal year. This follows $4.6 million accrued last year. This is all aimed at achieving $10 million in annual run rate savings moving forward.

Lastly, management has decided to stock up on inventory. As you can tell by looking at the chart above, using figures from the second quarter of each of the last few fiscal years, inventories are at the highest point they have been since the second quarter of the 2023 fiscal year. Year over year, they are up 15.4%, or $28.3 million. Management did this in order to deal with tariffs on goods ahead of time. It is worth noting, however, that some of the increase in inventories is solely because of the higher costs coming from tariffs. Management pegged this number at about $4.6 million. Regardless, it is good to see management working to address the challenges that the company faces. And if everything goes according to plan, this decision to ramp up inventory should cover the demand that the company has to contend with through the end of this year.

What's really frustrating is that this tariff and economic situation is impacting the company at a time when market conditions are finally stabilizing. In the chart above, you can see two different lines. The blue line shows an index representing watch prices up until the time my last article about the company was published. And the green line represents prices since then. As you can tell, prices have dropped significantly since the end of the COVID-19 pandemic when supply chain issues and general inflationary pressures caused prices to rise considerably. And now, they are more or less flatlined. This is great in and of itself. And it was one of the reasons why I was bullish about the company previously. But at a time when the pictures just now bottoming out, seeing the business be subjected to significant tariffs and as we enter into a recession does not bode well for confidence.

The good news is that if I am wrong about the direction of the economy and if tariffs eventually go away, the business will offer some nice upside potential. In the chart above, you can see how shares are priced using historical results from the 2025 fiscal year and estimates for 2026. Management unfortunately decided to not give out guidance for this year in light of the tariffs. So my 2026 estimates are basically a projection based on results that we have seen for the first half of the year. These multiples are indicative of a company that is slightly undervalued.

In the chart below, I also compared Movado Group to three similar firms, and while their pricing is all over the map, it's at least not expensive for the most part compared to these other businesses on a price to operating cash flow basis. And on an EV to EBITDA basis, it is cheaper than the two firms that have positive readings.

Takeaway

Right now, I would say that Movado Group is an interesting prospect. In the event that we get additional clarity regarding the operational future of the business in light of tariffs and economic conditions, I do think that more upside than what we have seen would be justified. Thanks to the large net cash position that the business has and the inventories that it built up in anticipation of tariff related pain, I think that it will definitely weather this storm. But until clarity comes about, I think it would be best to downgrade it to a Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Li Auto: Highly Profitable EV Play
data.description:
Li Auto (NASDAQ:LI) missed estimates on the bottom and top line for its second-quarter on Thursday, despite the electric vehicle company maintaining high vehicle margins in its core manufacturing business. Li Auto, however, is seeing moderating top line growth and submitted a weak delivery forecast for the third fiscal quarter which disappointed investors. On the other hand, robust profitability limits downside risks with this specific EV stock, and since Li Auto still reports some of the highest vehicle margins in the industry, I believe the investment setup is still overall bullish for investors.

Previous rating

I recommended Li Auto as a strong buy in my last article -- A Top Growth Pick For 2025 -- because the electric vehicle company, together with BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), was (and still is) leading the EV market in terms of gross margins. Li Auto also remained widely profitable in the second fiscal quarter, but the EV firm unfortunately guided for a weak delivery volume for Q3'25 which may create some negative sentiment overhang in the short term. I believe the company's high margins and general profitability outweigh any risks associated with moderating top line and delivery growth, and I continue to recommend the EV company to growth investors as a strong buy.

Strong margin profile and profitability in the core manufacturing business

Li Auto missed on both the bottom and top line in the second-quarter, although the EV enterprise remained profitable: the electric vehicle company generated $0.19 per-share in normalized earnings in Q2, missing the consensus estimate by $0.05 per-share. Second-quarter revenue totaled $4.24B which missed the average estimate by $206M.

In the second-quarter, the electric vehicle maker generated a total top line of 30.2B Chinese Yuan ($4.2B), showing a decline rate of 4.5% year-over-year. The EV company delivered 111,074 electric vehicles in Q2'25, showing only 2.3% growth compared to the year-earlier period.

Li Auto's slowing growth is due in part due to the company seeing increasing competition in the Chinese EV market, weaker demand for its L-Series EREV crossovers (EREV stands for Extended-Range EV) and a shift in consumer preferences towards lower-cost EV brands. However, Li Auto is launching new products which could help the electric vehicle maker improve its delivery trajectory going forward.

In July, the EV company debuted the Li i8, a six-seat all-electric SUV, in a bid to expand its BEV product portfolio. Li Auto began Li i8 deliveries on August 20, 2025 and the firm's management has laid out an ambitious target of ramping up deliveries to between 8,000 and 10,000 units by the end of September.

In July, Li Auto delivered 30,731 vehicles, showing a year-over-year decline of 40% and it was the second consecutive monthly delivery decline on a Y/Y basis for the EV company. Li Auto’s weak delivery performance lately is in part due to a weak market reception for the company’s L-Series -- which includes the L6, L7, L8 and L9 premium SUVs. Sales of these vehicles contracted between 40-53% year-over-year in July, showing a challenging situation for the EV company as far as the delivery trajectory is concerned.

On the other hand, the firm’s Mega EV, a battery-powered luxury minivan and multi-purpose vehicle, saw its deliveries surge to 2,816 units in July, showing a year-over-year growth rate of 330%.

While the delivery situation is challenged, the profitability picture looks very solid. Li Auto generated vehicle margins of 19.4% in Q2'25, showing a quarter-over-quarter decline of only 0.4 PP. For comparison, XPeng (XPEV), which already reported second-quarter earnings more than a week ago, disclosed vehicle margins of 14.3%. XPeng generated 3.8 PP margin growth in the second-quarter, on a sequential basis, so while the EV start-up did better than Li Auto in terms of growth, Li Auto still has by far the highest vehicle margins. NIO (NIO), which has not yet reported second-quarter results, had a vehicle margin of 10.2% in the first-quarter.

Overall, Li Auto maintained high levels of profitability as well, despite delivery headwinds. In Q2'25, the EV maker generated 1.5B Chinese Yuan ($204.9M) in non-GAAP earnings, showing 45% year-over-year growth.

Li Auto’s weak Q3 guidance

In terms of deliveries, Li Auto guided for a delivery volume of 90-95k units for the third-quarter, implying a drop-off rate of up to 41.1% year-over-year. The outlook was a disappointment and indicates that competitive pressures in the Chinese EV market are increasing. In the short term, the poor guidance for Q3'25 could create new negative sentiment overhang for shares of Li Auto... which I believe investors should see as an engagement opportunity.

Li Auto’s valuation

As I have argued many times in my previous coverage on the automaker, I see the company as top value in the Chinese electric vehicle start-up group. This is mainly due to Li Auto leading its rivals in terms of vehicle margins, due to its focus on higher-priced premium EVs. While the firm's top line and delivery growth is slowing, the EV company is widely profitable and seeing significant earnings expansion.

Currently, shares of Li Auto are priced at a forward (FY 2026) P/S ratio of 0.82X which makes the company cheaper than both XPeng and NIO. NIO lags its EV rivals in terms of vehicle margins (as discussed above), but the company is seeing strong momentum for its Onvo-branded EVs and has potential to double its valuation given its low price-to-revenue ratio of 0.9X.

I recently up-graded shares of XPeng to buy as well as the company is benefiting from both strong delivery growth as well as a margin expansion in its core business: Serious Margin Gains. If Li Auto were to revalue to its historical P/S ratio of 1.25X, then shares of the EV company would have a fair value in the neighborhood of $35.70/share... which is approximately 54% above the firm's current trading price. In order for shares to revalue higher, however, Li Auto would likely have to return to delivery growth (which the Li i8 could potentially deliver).

Risks with Li Auto

The biggest risk for Li Auto is a potentially disappointing delivery trajectory for the Li i8 SUV which is just starting to ramp up deliveries to a monthly run-rate of up to 10k units by the end of September. Another potential risk for the EV maker is that demand for electric vehicles has been shifting towards lower-cost EVs which could weigh on margins going forward and undermine the firm's position in the high-margin premium segment. However, because of its solid profitability, I believe that Li Auto is in the best position to absorb margin pressure. What would therefore change my mind about the EV maker is if Li Auto were to see a deterioration of its profitability profile or if Li i8 delivery projections didn't materialize.

Final thoughts

Li Auto submitted a decent earnings scorecard for the second fiscal quarter although the company missed on both earnings and revenue. While Li Auto faces some growth challenges and the Q3'25 delivery outlook was not especially great, the EV enterprise has a major advantage compared to many other EV plays, including those based in the U.S.: Li Auto is already widely profitable and is generating some of the highest vehicle margins in the industry which puts the company in a strong competitive position if margin pressures escalate. Further, Li Auto is also the cheapest electric vehicle play in the industry group, which is what I believe drastically limits investment risks for EV investors.

data.title: We Are Buying ECC Perpetual Preferred Stock Again
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

CLO stocks are among the favorite asset classes and popular among retail investors. Eagle Point Credit Co LLC (NYSE:ECC) continues to attract attention, despite high fees and the relatively low risk-adjusted return offered by its common stock. We're not fans of ECC's common stock, but we find its perpetual preferred stock very attractive. Today, we'll show you why we're once again betting on the perpetual preferred stock (NYSE:ECC.PR.D), which offers a yield of over 8%.

Company Overview

Founded in 2014 and headquartered in Greenwich, Connecticut, USA, Eagle Point Credit Company is a company that invests primarily in equity and junior debt tranches of collateralized loan obligations, or CLOs. ECC has approximately $1.52 billion in total assets, spread across 306 different investments. The company’s current market capitalization is approximately $928 million, with a leverage level of around 37% and an expense ratio of 10.51%, of which 2.15% is interest expenses and 2.06% is the management fees. The annualized distribution rate on NAV is close to 23%. Below is shown the ECC's portfolio of investments:

The credit quality of ECC is around a B score:

ECC’s common stock is trading at approximately $7.34 at the time of writing. However, its long-term yield has been disappointing compared to the fixed-income instruments that the company offers. Therefore, our focus today is on the perpetual preferred stock ECC.PR.D.

Our Pick

ECC.PR.D is currently trading at around $19.53, below par, and offers a current yield of 8.65%. It pays an annual cumulative dividend of 6.75%, which equates to $0.140625 per month or $1.6875 per year. ECC issued 1 million shares of ECC.PR.D with a par value of $25 each on 11/17/2021. The stock has a call date of 11/29/2026, with 1.25 years to call, and was rated BBB by Egan-Jones Ratings Co. at the time of its initial offering, although it is not rated by Moody’s, S&P, or Fitch. Below are the basic metrics of the stock:

Why do we choose ECC.PR.D?

  • -With an 8.65% CY and price below par, ECC.PR.D outperforms the historical real NAV return of ECC's common stock. ECC common stock often attracts attention for its high dividends, but they come with high fees and a lower return relative to risk.
  • -As a preferred stock, ECC.PR.D has priority over common stock in dividend distributions and in the event of liquidation.
  • - The company's leverage restrictions provide additional security for preferred stock investors.
  • - If we look at this chart based on current yields, with ECC.PR.D and its peers (fund stocks), we can see ECC.PR.D outperforms with the highest current yield:

ECC-D is supposed to have the highest current yield, and there is no doubt that having CLO equity as an asset is way riskier than having exchange-traded common stocks, as many of the other CEFs. But given the safety of the CEF structure and the regulatory leverage restrictions, we are ready to take on extra risk to boost our returns in a diversified portfolio.

Conclusion

Although Eagle Point Credit Company's common stock attracts investors with its high dividends, it comes with high fees and a lower risk-adjusted return. Instead, we recommend the perpetual preferred stock ECC.PR.D, which offers an attractive current yield of 8.65% and is trading below par. With priority in dividend distributions and liquidation, as well as the added security of the company's leverage restrictions, ECC.PR.D is a better alternative for investors looking for high and stable income. Compared to other similar stocks, ECC.PR.D has the highest current yield, making it attractive for investors willing to take extra risk and especially for those willing to take the risk of the common stock.

data.title: GoDaddy: Bookings And Renewal Pressure Underscore An Expensive Valuation (Downgrade)
data.description:
As we exit the bulk of the Q2 earnings season, the stock market is retreating nervously after closing near all-time highs. In my view, this is a clear signal that investors need to be focused on individual stock-picking. The stocks in our portfolios have to meet a high dual bar: high quality, and a reasonable valuation that leaves room for upside.

GoDaddy (NYSE:GDDY), the web hosting platform, is neither of these things. The company has been touting the higher attach rates on its AI product, GoDaddy Airo: but at the same time, the company hasn't been able to lift growth from its current high single digit pace, indicating that AI isn't really an accelerant for the company. And yet despite this, GoDaddy still trades at a premium to peers. Investors, meanwhile, are beginning to catch on to GoDaddy's relative lack of substance, with the stock declining by ~25% since the start of the year, with losses picking up steam after the company's recent Q2 earnings release.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on GoDaddy in June, when the stock was trading higher at $175 per share. Even though GoDaddy has declined nearly 20% since then, I'm not tempted at all to buy the dip in this name. I'm discouraged by GoDaddy's deceleration in its bookings growth rate, though I will acknowledge that the company has done a good job at keeping expense growth in check and boosting its adjusted EBITDA profile. I'm downgrading GoDaddy to a sell rating.

To me, there are a number of red flags and risk factors that investors need to be cognizant of here:

  • GoDaddy is seeing slowing bookings. It's difficult to claim that AI is an accelerant and a game-changer for your company, when the same company is also seeing bookings growth decelerate to the single digits. In my view, GoDaddy is simply treading water and claiming AI-powered features, while in reality it's not driving any meaningful new business from these releases.
  • Web hosting is a commoditized industry with little differentiation and plenty of competition. Web hosting sites may vary slightly on UI and features, but they basically all serve the same commoditized service. GoDaddy is among the slowest-growing of the group, compared to competitors like Wix and Shopify (SHOP).
