  • Heavy SMB exposure amid a choppy macroeconomy: Since a good chunk of GoDaddy's business lies in entrepreneurs and small businesses, the tough macroeconomy and higher possibility of business failures may impact GoDaddy's retention rates.
  • Churn issues: GoDaddy is seeing its customer base shrink on a y/y basis, reflecting the company's difficulties with retaining paid subscribers: which could be a reflection of sharp competition, the macro, or both.
  • Net debt position: Unlike most tech companies, GoDaddy has a substantial debt load that far outweighs its cash, which limits its financial flexibility.

Despite its many risks, I find GoDaddy to still trade at an unbearable premium. At current share prices near $148, GoDaddy trades at a market cap of $20.43 billion. After we net off the $1.09 billion of cash against $3.79 billion of debt on GoDaddy's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $23.13 billion.

For the remainder of FY25, GoDaddy has guided to $4.89-$4.94 billion in revenue, or 7-8% growth (slightly higher at the low end relative to a prior wider outlook of $4.86-$4.94 billion, or 6-8% growth). It has also indicated the same +100bps of NEBITDA ("normalized" EBITDA, equivalent to other companies' adjusted EBITDA) expansion, or a 31% margin that now points to $1.52 billion in nominal NEBITDA.

For next year FY26, Wall Street analysts are expecting GoDaddy to generate $5.28 billion in revenue (a continuation of 7% growth). If we assume a further 100bps margin expansion to 32%, normalized EBITDA next year would be $1.69 billion (+11% y/y). This puts GoDaddy's valuation multiples at:

  • 4.7x EV/FY25 revenue and 4.4x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 15.2x EV/FY25 NEBITDA and 13.7x EV/FY26 NEBITDA

My continued concern with GoDaddy is that it's unfavorably positioned against its closest pure-play rival, Wix. GoDaddy trades at a slight premium in revenue terms against Wix. On an adjusted EBITDA basis, GoDaddy's ~15x multiple is ~10% lower than Wix - but Wix is also growing revenue in the low teens, at nearly double the pace of GoDaddy - so arguably Wix deserves that healthy premium.

All in all, I'm not convinced to pay a mid-teens EBITDA multiple for a company that is struggling to maintain high single digit revenue growth in a crowded, commoditized industry. I'd prefer to wait for GoDaddy's valuation to keep correcting downward (while rival Wix has been appreciating higher) before stepping in.

Q2 download

Let's now go through GoDaddy's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

GoDaddy's revenue grew 8.3% y/y to $1.22 billion, slightly ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $1.21 billion (+7.3% y/y) by a one-point margin. Revenue kept pace with Q1's 7.7% growth rate: but where we are slightly concerned is the fact that GoDaddy's bookings of $1.35 billion grew only 6.6% y/y, underperforming revenue growth and decelerating 130bps versus 7.9% growth in Q1.

Ultimately, it is bookings that represents the longer-term arc of GoDaddy's growth trajectory: and the deceleration in bookings growth is a negative signal for the company's sales momentum. GoDaddy views one of its core functions as "bringing AI to micro businesses" - but despite the company's insistence that Airo adoption is increasing, the company isn't really seeing any benefits in monetization.

Management notes as well that macro conditions will put pressure on a spate of larger-client renewals later this year. Per CFO Mark McCaffrey's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

On total customers, we have largely moved beyond the impacts of eliminating deep discounts and divestitures. That said, we continue to see some residual pressure from migrations as they move through their initial renewal cycles. Excluding this remaining headwind, customer count has grown in each of the last 2 months. As these pressures subside and we benefit from strong conversion of higher intent cohorts, we expect a return to customer growth later this year."

To that note, we highlight the fact that GoDaddy's count of 20.4 million customers declined by -2% y/y, though the company insists it can return to growth by the end of the year. Another headwind to note: starting later this year, GoDaddy will no longer operate as a registry service provider for ".co" domains, which the company says will negatively impact revenue and bookings growth by -50bps starting in the fourth quarter.

Where GoDaddy is offering a bit of compensation is on stronger margins. The company's normalized EBITDA grew 15% y/y to $381.7 million:

EBITDA margins improved 180bps y/y to 31.3%, driven by economies of scale on GoDaddy's raised pricing for its core subscriptions, and well ahead of the company's target of expanding margins at a 100bps y/y clip in FY25.

Key takeaways

To me, GoDaddy's ~15x forward adjusted EBITDA multiple is a tall ask for a company that is generating only single-digit revenue and bookings growth (of which a portion is being driven by pricing increases, and where customer counts are actually declining). In my view, we have to be very selective in stock-picking in today's market, and GoDaddy is both expensive and of questionable quality: in other words, it doesn't make the cut for my portfolio.

data.title: Mastercard: Moat Is Under Fire With Potentially Easing Or Tighter Regulation
data.description:
Mastercard (NYSE:MA) is one of the world's leading payment networks, as it fully benefits as the world transitions from cash to card, as cards would have a payment network such as Mastercard. They don't issue cards directly, but provide the infrastructure that connects the consumer to the merchant (or any other institution). Every time a Mastercard-branded card is used in a transaction, Mastercard earns a fee for routing and authorizing the transaction. The duo of Mastercard and Visa is indispensable, as card payments all require the network that they provide. However, with an already high valuation and adoption due to its moat and potentially high regulation, Mastercard's future could be quite foggy especially in a pro-crypto/tokenization society. Hence, I'm rating Mastercard at a 'Hold'.

Thesis

As the world continues to have a fundamental switch in payments from cash to cards, Mastercard continues to grow at a good pace. With Mastercard's moat, it is able to continue to dominate the network scene alongside Visa. Mastercard is a compound machine, as alongside double-digit growth and a small dividend rate, it is a top choice as a defensive stock in one's portfolio. Growth continues to accelerate, as the tariff headwinds in Q2 earnings have shown that the effects have been minimal, as guidance from the start of the year has not been changed or affected.

However, as the world's adoption of card payments is getting more and more saturated, further growth acceleration for Mastercard and upside may seem limited despite its moat. With regulations and many antitrust lawsuits, Mastercard is under pressure as increased regulations could negatively affect margins.

Furthermore, with the potential adoption of crypto payments and tokenization, the next shift in society could be towards using digital currency as payment, which avoids Mastercard's networks. With a high valuation already, any signs of its moat eroding or higher regulations would suggest the valuation is not justified. As President Trump favors Crypto, even launching his own coin, further deregulation of digital currency payments during his tenure of presidency could affect Mastercard's revenue in the US, which could lead to worldwide adoption if it is successful and scales up.

Mastercard's Moat

Society has shifted from cash payments to card payments. In the UK, cash was used for 51% of payments in 2013 with the number falling to 12% in just 2023. In the US, cash was used in 11% of in-store transactions. 82% of US adults hold at least 1 credit card, as card payments are simply the norm now in society.

Mastercard has a wide moat, as a huge reason is with Mastercard's and Visa's dominance in the whole network itself, as they account for 90% of all payment processing (excluding China). Mastercard connects the cardholder (consumer), through the cardholder's issuer (bank) that provides the cards, to the merchant's bank (merchant). Once a card is used on a card machine, it gets processed through the Mastercard network, as it routes to the issuer's bank for approval. Issuer checks for whether there are sufficient funds, sends a response back to the Mastercard network, which then ensures the funds are settled between both banks (consumer and merchant). Mastercard alongside Visa is indispensable in today's society, with many major cities becoming more and more cashless such as Central London where tourists flood the city.

Mastercard has a huge network effect. Acting as a neutral entity to settle and guarantee payments daily, it reduces the risk of fraud or each issuing bank trusting the other individually. They trust the payment processors like Visa and Mastercard instead. Mastercard operates in 210 countries with tens of millions of merchants using Mastercard's services. The number was around 37 million in 2023. Mastercard's strong network effect lies in its system being available worldwide. A consumer who has a card through Mastercard can only use the card if they accept the Mastercard processing system. As more and more countries adopt the use of Mastercard, the issuer's card can be used in more and more countries worldwide. This leads to users having an easier experience, as they wouldn't require cash payments in a foreign country as Mastercard as a processing system is accepted. If Mastercard is expanded to more and more countries, all users whose system runs through Mastercard will have a better experience if they travel to these countries as their card would work. For example, American Express (AXP) is not taken as often as Mastercard in the UK, due to having higher fees. This gives AXP users more limitations on where to use their card, since it is not as globally accepted as Mastercard in my view.

Mastercard's Regulation Scrutiny Eroding Its Moat

Mastercard also has a strong moat with its intangible assets and switching costs. This is one of the biggest dual-edged swords for Mastercard and Visa. It has huge brand trust and awareness, whilst switching costs to a competitor like AXP or operating in cash would be more expensive or miss out on potential revenue. However, is there any other choice for merchants to choose other than Mastercard and Visa? They dominate the market, as major cities taking Mastercard and Visa is now the norm. Since operating payment processing requires a huge network, such as operating in many countries or as many locations as possible, which also requires trust from the issuers initially, is simply impossible for competition to come in as they run the whole network (AXP can compete with Visa and Mastercard as they require the bank's trust, as they distribute the cards themselves whilst running the systems).

Since there is no other system to switch to, Visa and Mastercard tend to come under scrutiny as they are highly regulated, so fees are controlled. This protects the consumer, as with higher fees, merchants tend to pass the cost down to the consumer to protect margins, which increases the price of today's cost of living.

Mastercard faces litigation globally, coming from the UK, EU, Australia, and the US. These tend to be surrounding their interchange fees. A lot more details can be found within their 10-Q, but the idea is this could potentially cost Mastercard billions. There have been claims as high as £14B for a single claim, though only settled for £200M, the risk and fear will linger. If the US follows with lowering interchange fees like the EU with so much litigation pressure, it could indirectly affect Mastercard.

The EU (UK retained this law after Brexit) as a general rule capped the interchange fee of a transaction to 0.2% for debit card and 0.3% for credit card transactions (UK cross-border to EU countries payments are higher and are also under investigation). The USA is significantly different, with interchange fees as high as ~3%. How does this actually affect Mastercard if interchange fees are regulated?

A lot of the negative effects are directly on the card issuers themselves, as Mastercard gets the indirect effects. After the EU regulation, EU issuers are estimated to lose between €5–6 billion in interchange revenues. In the US, the high interchange fee leads to credit cards with rewards, as reward levels were decreased in the EU. This leads to an incentive for consumers to spend, as a key issue that is brought up in Congress is that Visa and Mastercard's monopolistic behaviors (higher interchange fees and fees for merchants) lead to an increasing credit card balance, as the debt per household continues to increase.

A lot of the credit card balance is influenced by high interest rates which are not regulated (not related to Mastercard), but the high rates set on the merchants force them to increase prices, which doesn't protect the customer, as it was noted in Congress that small businesses may have no choice but to pay the fee as Visa and Mastercard dominate the market. This doesn't put a good reputation on Mastercard in my opinion, as I think it comes to a point where either growth slows down for Mastercard since the gross dollar volume will fall since credit card debt is too high and individuals struggle to pay it off, or interchange fees getting capped, which will affect the gross dollar volume as consumers will spend less (assuming prices fall and consumers spend the same).

A lot of the idea doesn't affect Mastercard directly, but with prices in the US increasing with tariff pressure, as well as downward revisions on non-farm payrolls, the level of credit card balance for the average household gets to a point before it breaks. To avoid an even higher balance, consumers spend less, which affects Mastercard. The average balance is $6580, 46% of households have a carried balance, with revolving credit card debt hitting a record of $1.18 trillion, which is worrying. Mastercard's credit cards are approximately 25% of transactions in the US, as I argue that as the credit card balance continues to rise, in my opinion, regulation or some type of limit is inevitable, which is negative sentiment for Mastercard, as gross dollar volume will be affected as consumers might start spending less. Other measures like capping interest rates may start to take place as Senator Hawley advocates it, which doesn't affect Mastercard, but more extreme measures could affect gross dollar volumes, which could affect Mastercard.

Mastercard is one of the firms likely at the center of attention by regulators. Mastercard has a wide moat, as without Mastercard payment processing systems (and Visa), the world today would be impossible to operate if there were no payment processing system. It continues to face continuous scrutiny as catalysts are minimal. If regulation does start to increase and gross dollar volume does show signs of slowing down, Mastercard's revenue faces significant headwind as its revenue is in its payment network.

Mastercard is not Saturated yet

Mastercard continues to get more and more saturated as there are minimal growth catalysts except for increased consumer spending, which is already a problem in the US. The amount of Mastercard cards only increased by 6%, which isn't that impressive with revenues up 16% and a valuation 3x of the sector median.

Use of card payments with Mastercard isn't fully saturated yet, as many locations in the world like South East Asia are starting to adopt card/digital payments. Places like Asia Pacific, the Middle East and Africa (APMEA) are growing at faster rates than the US where Mastercard is already established, as APMEA grew 21% in 2 years whilst the US grew only 13.97%. Even though Europe is Mastercard's biggest overseas segment, it grew by 26.6% over 2 years, which shows that the use of card payments isn't all saturated yet, as consumers are still shifting to card payments. However, if the purchase transactions show signs of slowing down, such as only increasing at the inflation rate or the % increase in disposable income, it shows that there are no new adopters of cards that use Mastercard.

Easing Regulation also affects Mastercard

GENIUS Act allows the existence of stablecoins, which is a cryptocurrency that are backed by assets that are considered reliable. A key example is Circle's (CRCL) USDC, which is 1:1 to the US dollar.

Mass adoption hasn't started yet and I'm not saying that using stablecoins as payment will be the norm with a society switch from card to stablecoins, but the existence and acceptance of stablecoins being passed by the senate threatens Mastercard's business model, as it avoids the whole Mastercard network. As long as President Trump is President, further easing of regulations of digital currency will happen as the fear will continue to linger for Mastercard in my opinion. This fear will continue in Trump's tenure, and the fear will only go away if mass adoption (or a % of society) doesn't occur in the short term, assuming the next president is not pro-Crypto. An example is Trump trying to push non-traditional assets like Crypto as a possibility to be invested into individuals' retirement savings, which I believe shows his push towards the adoption of Crypto. If the popularity of stablecoins increases or forms of tokenization, Mastercard's business model is directly under fire.

Cryptocurrency like Bitcoin isn't losing popularity either. A recent survey from Deloitte with North American CFOs showed some very interesting opinions of the executives. 1% did not envision using cryptocurrency for business functions in the long-term, organizations with revenue upwards of $10B had 40% of their CFOs believe that their treasury department will utilize crypto for investments/payments within 2 years, with the main concern for the executives being price volatility.

Industry regulation and security were huge concerns, being 40% and 36%, which are also huge concerns in the general population as crypto is not yet believed to be safe or reliable as it is new to society, with a survey in 2024 showing that 63% of Americans have ways to invest, use or trade currency in ways that are safe/reliable. Despite fears, US cryptocurrency payment users continue to rise, estimating a jump of 82% from 2024 to 2026.

The main takeaway is that crypto adoption is not slowing down, the bottom line being in the US, as the US wants to lead the world by being the first to mass adopt crypto as payments rather than just another tradable security. As more and more users adopt crypto, it gets more and more likely that payments will start shifting towards crypto. This directly affects Mastercard's gross dollar volume, though the extent cannot be known yet with crypto adoption just starting, it will affect Mastercard's growth as crypto completely avoids Mastercard's network.

With the US being at 30.9% of the gross dollar volume, the threat of crypto is very real and will affect Mastercard's revenue as it is a significant portion of its revenue. If crypto adoption in the US is successful, there are also possibilities that other countries will also start to adopt crypto, as a key advantage is sending money abroad, since Mastercard's network would have fees or payment delays/lag by the issuer. Time will tell whether crypto will be a huge threat to Mastercard's networks and revenue, but it is seemingly shifting that way.

Mastercard's Valuation and Index Comparison

This is Mastercard's Valuation Metrics:

From a valuation standpoint in its relative sector, Mastercard is overvalued from a comparison based on the numbers. However, investors have to remember that Mastercard has a moat over its sector, and it essentially runs a duopoly with Visa. It deserves a high P/E and a premium to the sector as it is essentially 1 of 2 firms who have networks globally/worldwide to connect the consumer to the merchant, as it is unreasonable to compare Mastercard to its sector when only 2 firms have such dominance in the world in payment processing, which are Visa and Mastercard. However, I would argue that Mastercard is slightly overvalued compared to its peer Visa.

Here is a comparison of P/E GAAP with Mastercard and its peers:

Here is a comparison of P/S with Mastercard and its peers:

The key trend is set, that Visa and Mastercard tend to trade at a deserving premium to their sector, as it trades at a premium to the only other payment processing system that has a considerable market share, American Express. Both firms have similar moats, as they both heavily rely on the payment network system, which is evident from the heavily similar P/E and P/S.

On a P/E basis, Mastercard would have to fall by ~14% to get to Visa's P/E GAAP basis. On a non-GAAP basis, it would have to fall by ~17% to get to Visa's P/E non-GAAP level. On a P/S basis, Mastercard would have to fall by around ~7% to get to Visa's level.

I'd argue that the premium of Visa and Mastercard to American Express and PayPal or payment firms is fully justified, as their moat extends globally and is relied upon daily by consumers and merchants. However, I argue that the bottom line is that Mastercard doesn't deserve a premium over Visa. Visa expects net revenue growth of high single digits to low double digits, while Mastercard's net revenue is expected to grow at a low teens rate. This doesn't justify an overvaluation of ~7%-~17% depending on the metric compared. This may also signal that Visa is undervalued, but Mastercard is already overvalued by 3x to the relative sector, which is quite significant. Mastercard is also trading at slightly higher levels than its average and median P/E, which sits currently at ~37.15.

Mastercard is a compounding machine, but the growth rate has to be questioned for long-term holders. I'm cherry picking the metric that Mastercard has underperformed the S&P500 on a 5Y scale, despite outperforming on any other long-term scale. It shows that further compounding for Mastercard may be foggy, considering the huge risk it is facing today. It outperformed as revenue grew as fast as card adoption was accelerating, but it is already quite saturated now as the next shift is towards crypto, which Mastercard has no big part of. It performed nearly 2x on a 10Y basis, but questions have to be raised as card adoption is slowing down due to saturation and a shift to crypto will affect gross dollar volumes, which will affect Mastercard in the long term.

Conclusion

Mastercard will continue to grow as long as spending with cards through Mastercard's networks continues to be the norm in society, but the risk of crypto is only getting bigger and bigger every day that Trump is president. For a long-term hold, Mastercard is not a bad hold, as it is highly unlikely that globally people will just stop using cards that have Mastercard's network, but the risk of easing or tighter regulation both affecting Mastercard from different angles is only getting larger every day. With limited upside as it is unlikely that Mastercard gets a higher valuation, being a lot higher than Visa's valuations and already higher than its historical valuations, Mastercard remains at a 'Hold'.
